sections.plain_summary: Migraine is a neurological condition characterised by recurring attacks of moderate-to-severe head pain, often on one side, accompanied by nausea, light sensitivity, and sound sensitivity. Attacks can last 4–72 hours and are frequently disabling. It is far more than a bad headache — it involves complex changes in brain activity, neurotransmitter signalling, and blood vessel behaviour. Many people live with migraine for years without identifying their specific triggers or finding a management approach that meaningfully reduces attack frequency.
sections.detailed_summary:
Migraine is a primary neurological disorder involving episodic dysfunction of the trigeminal pain pathways, cortical spreading depression, and dysregulation of serotonin, calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP), and other neurotransmitters. Attacks typically progress through distinct phases: a prodrome (hours to days before pain begins, involving mood changes, food cravings, fatigue, or yawning), an aura phase (present in roughly 25–30% of people, involving reversible neurological symptoms such as visual disturbances, tingling, or speech difficulty), the headache phase itself, and a postdrome ('migraine hangover') of cognitive fog, fatigue, and residual sensitivity.

Migraine has a strong genetic component — first-degree relatives of people with migraine are 2–4 times more likely to also have it. Hormonal fluctuations, particularly the drop in oestrogen before menstruation, are a major driver in women, which partly explains why migraine is approximately three times more prevalent in women than men.

The condition sits on a spectrum. At one end, some individuals have infrequent episodic attacks; at the other, chronic migraine involves 15 or more headache days per month, with at least 8 meeting migraine criteria. Medication overuse headache (MOH) — a rebound pattern caused by frequent use of acute pain relievers — can transform episodic migraine into chronic migraine, representing one of the most important and underappreciated traps in migraine management.

A range of triggers are commonly reported — including sleep disruption, skipping meals, dehydration, alcohol (particularly red wine and beer), certain foods, hormonal shifts, weather changes, stress and stress letdown, strong sensory stimuli, and overexertion. Triggers are highly individual and often additive: a single trigger may not cause an attack, but two or three in combination frequently does.

Until recently, preventive treatment options were limited and often poorly tolerated. The development of CGRP-targeted therapies (both monoclonal antibodies and gepants) since 2018 has significantly changed the preventive landscape, offering targeted options for people with frequent or chronic migraine who do not respond to older preventives.

prevalence.summary: Migraine affects roughly 1 in 7 people worldwide, making it one of the most common neurological conditions and a leading cause of disability.
prevalence.estimate: Approximately 12–15% of the global population; around 1 in 5 women and 1 in 16 men
prevalence.notes: Chronic migraine (15+ headache days/month) affects an estimated 1–2% of the population. Migraine is consistently ranked among the top causes of years lived with disability globally by the WHO. Underdiagnosis is common, particularly in men and in populations with limited healthcare access. Many people self-manage or attribute attacks to 'tension headaches', meaning true prevalence may be higher than surveys capture.

subgroups.name: Migraine without aura
subgroups.description: The most common form, accounting for roughly 70–75% of migraine cases. Attacks involve the characteristic headache phase without preceding neurological symptoms.
distinguishing_features: No visual, sensory, or speech disturbances before the headache
Pain typically unilateral, pulsating, moderate-to-severe
Worsened by routine physical activity
Accompanied by nausea and/or photo/phonophobia

subgroups.name: Migraine with aura
subgroups.description: Affects 25–30% of people with migraine. Involves reversible focal neurological symptoms developing over 5–20 minutes and lasting under 60 minutes, followed by or concurrent with headache.
distinguishing_features: Visual aura most common: zigzag lines (scintillating scotoma), blind spots, flickering lights
Sensory aura: tingling or numbness spreading across face or hand
Speech/language aura: word-finding difficulty or slurred speech
Small increased stroke risk in women with aura who smoke or use combined oral contraceptives
Aura can occasionally occur without headache following (silent migraine)

subgroups.name: Chronic migraine
subgroups.description: Defined as 15 or more headache days per month for over three months, with at least 8 of those days meeting migraine criteria. Often develops from episodic migraine through a process called chronification.
distinguishing_features: High headache day burden severely impacts daily functioning and quality of life
Strongly associated with medication overuse headache (MOH)
More likely to co-occur with anxiety, depression, and sleep disorders
Requires preventive (not just acute) treatment as a priority
CGRP-targeted therapies and OnabotulinumtoxinA (Botox) specifically approved for chronic migraine

subgroups.name: Vestibular migraine
subgroups.description: A subtype characterised by recurrent episodes of vertigo, dizziness, or balance disturbance associated with migraine. Frequently misdiagnosed as inner ear disease or BPPV.
distinguishing_features: Vertigo or dizziness (spinning, rocking, or unsteady sensation) is the primary or prominent symptom
Headache may be absent during vestibular episodes
Motion sensitivity often extreme
Auditory symptoms (tinnitus, ear fullness) may be present, creating diagnostic confusion with Ménière's disease
Episodes can last minutes to days

subgroups.name: Hemiplegic migraine
subgroups.description: A rare, severe form in which aura includes motor weakness (hemiplegia) on one side of the body. Can be familial (genetic mutations in ion channel genes) or sporadic.
distinguishing_features: Temporary one-sided weakness or paralysis as part of aura
Can mimic stroke — requires careful differential diagnosis
Symptoms may last hours to days
Familial form linked to mutations in CACNA1A, ATP1A2, or SCN1A genes
Triptans and ergotamines generally contraindicated

subgroups.name: Menstrual migraine
subgroups.description: Attacks that occur predictably in relation to the menstrual cycle, typically in the window from 2 days before to 3 days after the start of menstruation, driven by the oestrogen drop.
distinguishing_features: Attacks often more severe, longer-lasting, and less responsive to treatment than non-menstrual attacks
Typically occur without aura
Predictable timing allows for mini-preventive strategies (e.g. frovatriptan or naproxen perimenstrually)
Perimenopause often worsens menstrual migraine before improvement post-menopause

subgroups.name: Medication overuse headache (MOH)
subgroups.description: A secondary headache disorder developing in people with a primary headache disorder who overuse acute medications, resulting in headache on 15+ days per month. Also called rebound headache.
distinguishing_features: Headache present on waking, often dull and daily
Acute medications provide diminishing relief over time
Associated with use of triptans >10 days/month, simple analgesics >15 days/month, or opioids/ergotamines >10 days/month
Preventive treatments are less effective while overuse continues
Requires a supervised withdrawal period — often results in significant improvement

symptoms.symptom: Unilateral throbbing or pulsating head pain
symptoms.notes: Pain is typically on one side but can be bilateral. Intensity is moderate to severe and worsens with routine physical activity such as climbing stairs.
Present in the majority of attacks. Vomiting can sometimes provide temporary pain relief. Nausea also slows gastric emptying, reducing absorption of oral medications.
Even ordinary indoor lighting can be intolerable during an attack. Sunlight is particularly problematic.
Normal sounds feel painfully loud. Many people retreat to quiet, dark environments.
Strong smells such as perfume, food, or cleaning products can trigger or worsen attacks. Often underreported.

symptoms.symptom: Visual aura — scintillating scotoma or zigzag lines
symptoms.notes: Classic visual aura involves a crescent of flickering zigzag lines that slowly expands across the visual field over 20–30 minutes. Highly characteristic of migraine with aura.
Typically spreads slowly from fingers up the arm or across one side of the face. Distinct from the sudden onset of stroke symptoms.

symptoms.symptom: Cognitive aura — word-finding difficulty or aphasia
symptoms.notes: Difficulty finding words or forming sentences during aura. Often alarming but reversible.

symptoms.symptom: Prodrome symptoms — fatigue, mood changes, food cravings, yawning
symptoms.notes: Can begin 24–48 hours before pain. Yawning and carbohydrate cravings are particularly characteristic. Recognising the prodrome can allow earlier treatment.

symptoms.symptom: Postdrome — cognitive fog, fatigue, head tenderness
symptoms.notes: Often called the 'migraine hangover'. Can last 24–48 hours after headache resolves. Cognitive impairment during this phase is significant and underrecognised.

symptoms.symptom: Allodynia — pain from normally non-painful stimuli
symptoms.notes: Skin tenderness during attacks, e.g. pain from combing hair, wearing glasses, or light touch to the scalp. Indicates central sensitisation and is associated with treatment resistance if not treated early.
Extremely common — reported in up to 75% of attacks. Often mistaken for a cervicogenic cause, but in migraine it is typically part of the attack itself via trigeminal-cervical nucleus involvement.
Particularly prominent in vestibular migraine. Can also occur as a minor feature of other migraine types.
Autonomic symptoms mediated by the trigeminal-autonomic reflex. Often leads to misdiagnosis as sinus headache — the vast majority of self-diagnosed 'sinus headaches' are actually migraine.

symptoms.symptom: Increased urination before or during attack
symptoms.notes: Part of the prodrome for some individuals. Related to hypothalamic activation.

symptoms.symptom: Cognitive difficulty / brain fog during attacks
symptoms.notes: Difficulty concentrating, slowed thinking, and memory lapses are common during and after attacks.

key_patterns: Recurring headache attacks lasting 4–72 hours, unilateral, pulsating, moderate-to-severe, with nausea or photo/phonophobia — this pattern meets ICHD-3 diagnostic criteria for migraine without aura
Presence of reversible focal neurological symptoms (visual, sensory, or speech) developing over 5–20 minutes before or during headache — consistent with migraine with aura
Predictable perimenstrual timing of attacks in women — suggests hormonal (menstrual) migraine subtype
Headache frequency escalating over months with acute medication use increasing in parallel — pattern consistent with medication overuse headache
Recurrent vertigo or dizziness with accompanying headache, light/sound sensitivity, or motion intolerance — pattern consistent with vestibular migraine
Detailed headache diary revealing trigger patterns, prodrome features, and attack frequency — foundational to diagnosis and management planning

at_home.name: Headache and migraine diary
at_home.description: Tracking each headache day with onset time, duration, pain intensity, associated symptoms (nausea, aura, light sensitivity), potential triggers (sleep, meals, stress, menstrual cycle, weather), and medications used. Apps such as Migraine Buddy, N1-Headache, or a simple paper diary work well. After 4–8 weeks, patterns often become visible — including menstrual links, weekend patterns, sleep disruption effects, and dietary triggers.
at_home.safety_notes: No risks. This is the single most useful thing individuals can do before a clinical appointment. A completed diary significantly improves the quality of any consultation.

at_home.name: Trigger elimination trial
at_home.description: Systematically removing one commonly reported trigger at a time (e.g. alcohol, artificial sweeteners, MSG, skipping meals) for 4–6 weeks while tracking attack frequency. Because triggers are often additive and individual, a structured diary approach is needed to draw valid conclusions.
at_home.safety_notes: Safe when approached methodically. Avoid eliminating multiple food groups simultaneously without guidance from a registered dietitian, as this can create nutritional deficits and increase anxiety around eating.

at_home.name: Sleep regularity experiment
at_home.description: Maintaining consistent sleep and wake times seven days a week — including weekends — for 4–6 weeks, and tracking whether attack frequency changes. Weekend lie-ins are a well-known migraine trigger ('weekend migraine').
at_home.safety_notes: No risks. This is one of the highest-yield lifestyle experiments for many people with migraine.

at_home.name: Hydration tracking
at_home.description: Ensuring consistent daily fluid intake (at least 1.5–2 litres of water, adjusted for body weight and activity) and noting whether dehydration correlates with attack onset. Tracking urine colour as a proxy for hydration status.
at_home.safety_notes: Generally safe. Individuals with kidney conditions or heart conditions should confirm fluid targets with their doctor.

clinical.name: Clinical diagnosis using ICHD-3 criteria
clinical.description: Migraine is a clinical diagnosis — there is no definitive biomarker or imaging test. Neurologists and headache specialists apply the International Classification of Headache Disorders (3rd edition) criteria based on history, attack characteristics, and exclusion of secondary causes. A detailed headache diary significantly aids this process.
clinical.availability: General practitioners can apply ICHD-3 criteria for straightforward cases. Neurologists or dedicated headache clinics are appropriate for complex, chronic, or treatment-refractory cases.

clinical.name: MRI brain (with and without contrast)
clinical.description: Not required for typical migraine, but indicated to exclude secondary causes when: headache pattern changes suddenly, neurological deficits are present, headache begins after age 50, or when 'thunderclap' onset is reported. Does not diagnose migraine but rules out structural causes.
clinical.availability: Ordered by GPs, neurologists, or emergency physicians depending on clinical context. May require referral.

clinical.name: Hormonal panel (oestrogen, progesterone, LH, FSH)
clinical.description: In women whose migraines are clearly menstrually linked or who are in perimenopause, hormonal profiling can help guide treatment decisions — particularly around hormonal preventive strategies or HRT considerations.
clinical.availability: Ordered by a GP, gynaecologist, or reproductive endocrinologist. Timing of testing in the cycle is important for interpretation.

clinical.name: Comprehensive blood panel (thyroid, iron, B12, inflammatory markers)
clinical.description: Used to exclude secondary contributors to headache frequency, such as hypothyroidism, anaemia, or systemic inflammation. Not diagnostic for migraine but important for complete workup, especially in treatment-refractory cases.
clinical.availability: Ordered by any GP. Often underperformed in people with established migraine diagnosis.

clinical.name: Videonystagmography (VNG) / vestibular function testing
clinical.description: In suspected vestibular migraine, formal vestibular testing helps differentiate from peripheral vestibular disorders (e.g. Ménière's disease, BPPV). Measures eye movements and balance responses to various stimuli.
clinical.availability: Performed by audiologists or ENT/otoneurology departments. Requires referral.

clinical.name: CGRP biomarker and calcitonin gene-related peptide assays
clinical.description: Research has established that CGRP levels are elevated during migraine attacks and in chronic migraine between attacks. While not yet in routine clinical use as a diagnostic tool, CGRP measurement underpins the development of CGRP-targeted preventive therapies and may eventually guide treatment selection.
clinical.availability: Research setting primarily. Not routinely available in clinical practice as a diagnostic test.

clinical.name: Neuropsychological testing
clinical.description: Formal cognitive assessment may be used in people with significant cognitive complaints during or between attacks, or to document functional impact. Particularly relevant in chronic migraine with high disability burden.
clinical.availability: Available through neuropsychologists; referral typically required.

related_conditions.name: Fibromyalgia
related_conditions.explanation: Migraine and fibromyalgia co-occur at significantly elevated rates, both involving central sensitisation — a state of amplified pain processing in the central nervous system. People with both conditions often experience more severe and treatment-resistant migraine.

related_conditions.name: Central sensitisation
related_conditions.explanation: Central sensitisation is both a feature of individual migraine attacks (allodynia during attacks) and a likely driver of migraine chronification. Repeated attacks may lower pain thresholds over time, creating a self-perpetuating cycle of increasing frequency.

related_conditions.name: POTS / dysautonomia
related_conditions.explanation: POTS and migraine co-occur more frequently than chance, particularly in young women. Both may involve dysregulation of the autonomic nervous system and blood flow to the brain. Orthostatic triggers — such as dehydration or prolonged standing — can precipitate both conditions.

related_conditions.name: hEDS / HSD
related_conditions.explanation: Migraine is significantly more prevalent in people with hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome and hypermobility spectrum disorder. The mechanism may involve autonomic dysfunction, cervical instability, or connective tissue changes affecting vascular tone.

related_conditions.name: Anxiety disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Anxiety and migraine have a bidirectional relationship — each increases the risk of the other. Anxiety heightens cortical excitability and stress reactivity, which may lower the threshold for attacks. Conversely, the unpredictability of migraine attacks generates significant anticipatory anxiety.

related_conditions.name: Depression
related_conditions.explanation: Depression is approximately 2–4 times more prevalent in people with migraine than the general population. The relationship is bidirectional and shares serotonergic, neuroinflammatory, and HPA axis mechanisms. Chronic migraine with high disability burden is particularly strongly associated with depression.

related_conditions.name: Endometriosis
related_conditions.explanation: Endometriosis and migraine share hormonal sensitivity (particularly to oestrogen fluctuation) and may share neuroinflammatory mechanisms. Both conditions are disproportionately common in women with heightened central pain processing.

related_conditions.name: Hormonal / menstrual cycle effects
related_conditions.explanation: Oestrogen withdrawal, particularly the pre-menstrual drop, is one of the most well-established migraine triggers. Hormonal fluctuations across the menstrual cycle, pregnancy, postpartum period, and perimenopause profoundly affect attack frequency and severity in many women.

related_conditions.name: Perimenopause
related_conditions.explanation: Migraine often worsens significantly during perimenopause due to erratic oestrogen fluctuations, before typically improving after menopause. Perimenopausal migraine escalation is commonly underappreciated and undertreated.

related_conditions.name: Poor sleep / insomnia
related_conditions.explanation: Sleep disruption is both a trigger and a consequence of migraine. Irregular sleep patterns, insufficient sleep, and sleeping too long all commonly precipitate attacks. Insomnia co-occurs with chronic migraine at high rates, creating a reinforcing cycle.

related_conditions.name: Cervical instability
related_conditions.explanation: Cervicogenic headache arising from upper cervical dysfunction can closely mimic migraine and frequently co-exists with it. The trigeminal-cervical nucleus processes signals from both cranial and upper cervical structures, meaning cervical dysfunction can modulate migraine threshold and frequency.

related_conditions.name: Temporomandibular disorder
related_conditions.explanation: TMD and migraine co-occur at elevated rates, sharing trigeminal pain pathways. Jaw tension and bruxism — which often worsen during stressful periods — can lower the migraine threshold and trigger attacks. Treating TMD may reduce migraine frequency in some people.

related_conditions.name: Post-concussion syndrome
related_conditions.explanation: Head injury can trigger the onset of migraine or significantly worsen pre-existing migraine. Post-traumatic headache following concussion often presents identically to migraine and is treated similarly, but requires its own diagnostic framework.

related_conditions.name: Binocular vision dysfunction
related_conditions.explanation: Binocular vision dysfunction causes eyestrain and visual stress that can trigger or closely mimic migraine. It is frequently missed because standard eye exams do not always assess binocular alignment adequately. A trial of prism correction can be diagnostic and therapeutic.

related_conditions.name: Chronic sinusitis
related_conditions.explanation: The overwhelming majority of self-diagnosed 'sinus headaches' are actually migraine — studies suggest up to 90% of people presenting with presumed sinus headache meet migraine criteria. Autonomic features of migraine (nasal congestion, watery eyes) mimic sinus symptoms.

related_conditions.name: ME / CFS
related_conditions.explanation: Migraine occurs at elevated rates in people with ME/CFS. Both conditions may involve neuroinflammation, autonomic dysfunction, and mitochondrial dysfunction. The postdrome of severe migraine attacks shares features with post-exertional malaise.

related_conditions.name: Histamine intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: Histamine is a potent vasodilator and can trigger migraine attacks in susceptible individuals. Some people with migraine notice that high-histamine foods (aged cheeses, red wine, fermented foods) are reliable triggers, suggesting histamine intolerance may be a contributing factor.

related_conditions.name: Mast cell activation syndrome
related_conditions.explanation: MCAS involves dysregulated mast cell release of histamine and other mediators, which can trigger migraine attacks. People with MCAS often have heightened reactivity to multiple migraine triggers including foods, scents, and environmental factors.

related_conditions.name: IBS
related_conditions.explanation: IBS and migraine co-occur at significantly elevated rates — likely reflecting shared mechanisms including central sensitisation, serotonin dysregulation, and heightened visceral and somatic pain processing. People with both conditions tend to have more severe presentations of each.

related_conditions.name: Long COVID
related_conditions.explanation: New-onset migraine and worsening of pre-existing migraine are commonly reported in long COVID. Neuroinflammation, autonomic dysfunction, and CGRP pathway dysregulation following COVID-19 infection are proposed mechanisms.

related_conditions.name: Dehydration
related_conditions.explanation: Dehydration is one of the most consistently reported and physiologically plausible migraine triggers. Even mild dehydration can reduce cerebral blood flow and alter osmotic balance, lowering the attack threshold in susceptible individuals.

related_conditions.name: MTHFR / methylation variants
related_conditions.explanation: MTHFR polymorphisms that impair folate metabolism and raise homocysteine levels have been associated with migraine with aura specifically. Elevated homocysteine may affect nitric oxide metabolism and endothelial function. High-dose riboflavin and methylfolate supplementation are sometimes used in people with these variants.

related_conditions.name: Mitochondrial dysfunction
related_conditions.explanation: Evidence suggests that impaired mitochondrial energy metabolism may contribute to cortical spreading depression and migraine susceptibility. This is supported by the therapeutic response some people have to mitochondrial-supportive supplements such as CoQ10 and riboflavin.

strategies.name: Triptans (acute treatment)
strategies.description: Serotonin (5-HT1B/1D) receptor agonists that constrict dilated cranial blood vessels and block trigeminal pain signalling. Most effective when taken early in an attack, before allodynia develops. Available as tablets, nasal sprays, and subcutaneous injections. Seven triptans are available; individuals may respond differently to different agents.
strategies.notes: Contraindicated in hemiplegic migraine, in people with cardiovascular disease, uncontrolled hypertension, or history of stroke. Using triptans more than 10 days/month risks medication overuse headache. Nasal sprays and injections act faster and bypass nausea-related absorption issues.

strategies.name: Gepants (acute and preventive treatment)
strategies.description: CGRP receptor antagonists available for acute treatment (ubrogepant, rimegepant) and preventive treatment (atogepant, rimegepant). Unlike triptans, gepants do not cause vasoconstriction, making them suitable for people with cardiovascular contraindications to triptans. Rimegepant taken every other day has shown preventive benefit.
strategies.notes: Gepants carry a lower risk of medication overuse headache than triptans or NSAIDs, though caution is still warranted with frequent use. Currently more expensive and availability varies by country.

strategies.name: CGRP monoclonal antibodies (preventive treatment)
strategies.description: Monthly or quarterly injectable preventives (erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab) or intravenous infusion (eptinezumab) targeting the CGRP pathway. Represent the first migraine-specific preventive class and are significantly more effective and better tolerated than older preventives for many people. Typically considered after 2–3 prior preventives have failed.
strategies.notes: Self-administered subcutaneous injections. Some people achieve complete or near-complete remission. Constipation is the main notable side effect with erenumab. Access may be limited by cost and insurance approval criteria.

strategies.name: Traditional preventive medications
strategies.description: Includes beta-blockers (propranolol, metoprolol), tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline, nortriptyline), anticonvulsants (topiramate, valproate), and calcium channel blockers (flunarizine). These are first-line preventives in most healthcare systems due to cost and availability, though side effect profiles limit tolerability for many people.
strategies.notes: Topiramate causes cognitive side effects ('dopamax') significant enough to discontinue in many people. Valproate is contraindicated in pregnancy. Individual response is highly variable. A 2–3 month adequate trial is typically needed before concluding a preventive is ineffective.

strategies.name: OnabotulinumtoxinA (Botox) injections
strategies.description: FDA and NICE approved specifically for chronic migraine (15+ headache days/month). Given as a series of 31 injections around the head and neck every 12 weeks by a trained clinician. Thought to work by blocking release of pain neurotransmitters from peripheral trigeminal nerve endings.
strategies.notes: Benefits accumulate over multiple treatment cycles — 2–3 cycles are typically needed before full effect. Most effective for chronic migraine; not approved for episodic migraine. Available through headache clinics and neurology departments.

strategies.name: NSAIDs and simple analgesics (acute treatment)
strategies.description: Naproxen sodium, ibuprofen, and aspirin (especially in combination with caffeine, e.g. Excedrin Migraine) can be effective for mild-to-moderate attacks. Indomethacin is particularly effective for certain migraine subtypes. Should be used as a backup to triptans for moderate-severe attacks.
strategies.notes: Overuse risk: analgesics used more than 15 days/month can cause medication overuse headache. Long-term NSAID use carries GI and renal risks. Caffeine can help absorption but caffeine withdrawal can itself trigger migraine.

strategies.name: Sleep regularity
strategies.description: Maintaining consistent sleep and wake times every day, including weekends, is one of the highest-impact lifestyle changes for many people with migraine. Both insufficient sleep and excessive sleep are common triggers. Addressing underlying insomnia is important.
strategies.notes: The 'weekend migraine' pattern — attacks on Saturday or Sunday morning — is often due to sleeping in. Gradual, incremental changes to sleep timing are more sustainable than abrupt shifts.

strategies.name: Regular meals and stable blood sugar
strategies.description: Skipping meals or prolonged fasting causes blood sugar drops that are well-established migraine triggers. Eating regular, balanced meals every 4–5 hours helps maintain metabolic stability. Reactive hypoglycaemia can mimic prodrome symptoms.
strategies.notes: Helpful to keep a small, easily digestible snack available for situations where meals may be delayed. Some people find low-carbohydrate or ketogenic diets helpful, though evidence is preliminary and individual responses vary.

strategies.name: Consistent hydration
strategies.description: Maintaining adequate daily fluid intake, with attention to increased needs in heat, exercise, or illness. Dehydration is a modifiable trigger that is often overlooked.
strategies.notes: Starting an attack with oral rehydration fluids (electrolyte solutions rather than plain water) may be more effective. Hydration alone rarely eliminates attacks but can reduce frequency in dehydration-susceptible individuals.

strategies.name: Riboflavin (Vitamin B2) supplementation
strategies.description: High-dose riboflavin (400mg/day) has been shown in multiple trials to reduce migraine frequency by approximately 50% in some individuals. Supports mitochondrial energy production (oxidative phosphorylation), addressing a proposed metabolic basis for migraine susceptibility.
strategies.notes: Very safe, low cost, urine turns bright yellow (harmless). Effects take 2–3 months to appear. More effective in some people than others — possibly those with mitochondrial energy deficits or MTHFR variants. Available over the counter.

strategies.name: Coenzyme Q10 (CoQ10) supplementation
strategies.description: 300–400mg/day CoQ10 has shown preventive effects in several trials, reducing attack frequency. Like riboflavin, it supports mitochondrial function. Some studies show elevated CoQ10 deficiency in children and adults with migraine.
strategies.notes: Well tolerated. Ubiquinol form may have better absorption than ubiquinone. Takes 3 months to adequately assess effect. Can be used alongside riboflavin and magnesium.

strategies.name: Magnesium supplementation
strategies.description: Magnesium deficiency has been identified in a subset of people with migraine, and magnesium plays a role in NMDA receptor function and cortical spreading depression. Oral magnesium glycinate or citrate (300–600mg elemental magnesium daily) is recommended by multiple headache societies as a safe preventive option.
strategies.notes: Magnesium oxide has poor bioavailability and causes diarrhoea. Glycinate or citrate forms are better tolerated. IV magnesium sulphate in emergency settings can abort acute attacks. Low serum magnesium may not reflect intracellular deficiency — red blood cell magnesium testing is more sensitive.

strategies.name: Biofeedback and neurofeedback
strategies.description: Biofeedback (particularly thermal and EMG biofeedback) has strong evidence for migraine prevention, with effect sizes comparable to first-line medications. Neurofeedback is more emerging. Both approaches help individuals recognise and regulate physiological states that precede attacks.
strategies.notes: Requires access to a trained practitioner and multiple sessions. Less available and often not covered by insurance, but durability of effects may be superior to medication. Particularly valuable for those wanting non-pharmacological options or in pregnancy.

strategies.name: Cognitive Behavioural Therapy (CBT)
strategies.description: CBT addresses the psychological components of chronic migraine — including pain catastrophising, avoidance behaviours, fear of attacks, and the bidirectional relationship between migraine and anxiety/depression. Strong evidence for reducing headache frequency and improving function.
strategies.notes: Most effective when delivered by a therapist with pain psychology training. Telehealth options have expanded access. Particularly important for people with chronic migraine and significant disability.

strategies.name: Stress management and relaxation training
strategies.description: Stress is among the most consistently reported migraine triggers. Paradoxically, the 'let-down' after intense stress (weekend, post-deadline) is also a trigger. Progressive muscle relaxation, diaphragmatic breathing, and mindfulness-based approaches can reduce attack frequency.
strategies.notes: Regular practice matters more than intensity. The goal is sustained reduction of baseline nervous system arousal, not emergency relaxation during an attack.

strategies.name: Aerobic exercise
strategies.description: Regular moderate aerobic exercise (150 minutes/week) has preventive effects comparable to some medications in several trials. Releases endorphins, reduces cortisol, and improves sleep. Starting exercise during an attack can worsen it, but regular exercise between attacks is preventive.
strategies.notes: Dehydration and exertion-induced vasodilation can trigger attacks, particularly during the first few weeks of starting a new exercise programme. Gradual ramping up with attention to hydration and electrolytes is advised.

strategies.name: Neuromodulation devices
strategies.description: Several non-invasive neuromodulation devices are FDA-cleared for migraine: transcutaneous supraorbital nerve stimulation (Cefaly), single-pulse transcranial magnetic stimulation (SpringTMS/sTMS), vagus nerve stimulation (gammaCore), and remote electrical neuromodulation (Nerivio). These offer non-pharmacological acute and preventive options.
strategies.notes: Cost and access vary significantly. Most appropriate for people wanting to minimise medication load, those with contraindications to medications, or those with incomplete response to standard treatments. Evidence strength varies by device.

strategies.name: Trigger identification and management
strategies.description: Systematically identifying personal triggers through diary tracking, then making targeted adjustments. Common modifiable triggers include irregular sleep, skipped meals, dehydration, excessive caffeine or caffeine withdrawal, alcohol, strong sensory stimuli, and known dietary triggers.
strategies.notes: Triggers are additive and individual. Overly restrictive trigger avoidance can increase sensitivity and anxiety. The goal is managing trigger load, not eliminating all possible triggers from life. Working with a headache specialist or psychologist helps strike the right balance.

things_to_avoid: Using acute medications (triptans, analgesics, NSAIDs) more than 10–15 days per month — this leads to medication overuse headache (MOH), a cycle where the medication itself perpetuates daily or near-daily headache
Irregular sleep schedules, including sleeping in on weekends — 'weekend migraine' is a direct consequence of this common pattern
Skipping meals or prolonged fasting — blood glucose drops are a potent and entirely avoidable trigger
Excess caffeine intake or abrupt caffeine withdrawal — both can trigger attacks; if reducing caffeine, doing so gradually over weeks
Opioid medications for routine migraine management — opioids are particularly prone to causing MOH, are less effective than triptans for migraine specifically, and increase the risk of chronification
Diagnosing and treating as 'sinus headache' without confirming a genuine sinus cause — the vast majority are migraine with autonomic features, and sinus treatments will be ineffective

Delaying acute treatment — waiting until pain becomes severe substantially reduces the effectiveness of triptans and allows allodynia (central sensitisation) to develop, making the attack harder to treat
Ignoring or dismissing the postdrome phase — pushing through postdrome with cognitive demands or physical activity can extend recovery and trigger a new attack

Over-restricting dietary triggers without professional guidance — leads to nutritional imbalances, increased food anxiety, and often does not meaningfully reduce attack frequency without a systematic approach

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Medication overuse headache is extremely common and is often the primary reason episodic migraine has become chronic — and the people most at risk are those who are trying hardest to treat their pain.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: It feels counterintuitive and unfair that the medications prescribed to relieve pain can cause more headache. People in daily pain rely on acute medications to function, and being told to take less medication when in pain feels impossible. Healthcare providers also often fail to explain this risk clearly.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who successfully identify MOH and complete a supervised withdrawal — usually 4–12 weeks — often experience a dramatic reduction in headache days, returning to an episodic pattern. This almost always requires support from a headache neurologist or specialist to manage the transition safely and introduce effective preventives simultaneously.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Most people with frequent migraine are not on any preventive treatment, and most who are trialled one preventive and stopped after experiencing side effects — never knowing that other options exist or that the newer CGRP-targeted therapies are substantially better tolerated.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Preventive treatment requires consistent daily medication with a 2–3 month lag before benefit, during which attacks continue. The older preventives (topiramate, amitriptyline) have burdensome side effects that lead many people to discontinue. After one or two failed trials, people and their doctors often give up on prevention.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Individuals who persist through trials of multiple preventive options — or who access CGRP monoclonal antibodies after two prior preventive failures — often achieve 50–80% reductions in monthly migraine days. Reaching this point typically requires a neurologist or headache specialist, not a general practitioner.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Lifestyle factors — particularly sleep regularity, meal timing, and stress management — have a measurable impact on migraine frequency that is often equal to or greater than medication, and most people have not fully implemented them.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Lifestyle advice can feel dismissive to people in significant pain, as if they are being blamed for their condition. The changes required (consistent bedtimes, never skipping meals, daily stress practices) also require ongoing effort without the immediate reward that acute medication provides.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who systematically implement sleep regularity, regular meals, consistent hydration, and a sustainable exercise routine alongside their acute and preventive medications typically report meaningfully fewer attacks, shorter durations, and reduced severity within 2–3 months. The cumulative effect of consistent lifestyle changes often outperforms any single medication.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Migraine is a condition of the brain's threshold — not just a series of individual attacks to be treated in isolation — and without addressing the underlying threshold (through prevention), acute treatments become progressively less effective.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: The acute pain of a migraine attack demands immediate relief, making it natural to focus on acute management. The concept of a 'brain threshold' is abstract and the benefits of preventive treatment are delayed and probabilistic rather than immediate and certain.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who shift their primary management focus from acute to preventive — tracking attack frequency, working with a specialist on a preventive regimen, and using acute treatments judiciously within monthly limits — consistently report the best long-term outcomes. The goal is fewer attacks to treat, not better tools to fight each attack.

key_levers.lever: Consistent preventive treatment with adequate duration and appropriate agent
key_levers.why_it_matters: Most people with frequent migraine (6+ days/month) require preventive treatment to achieve meaningful improvement. Acute-only management leads to treatment escalation, MOH, and chronification. A single failed preventive trial does not mean prevention doesn't work.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request a referral to a neurologist or dedicated headache clinic. Bring a completed 2–4 week headache diary to the first appointment. Ask specifically about CGRP-targeted options if older preventives have been trialled and failed.
key_levers.timeline: 2–3 months per preventive trial to assess effectiveness. CGRP monoclonal antibodies often show benefit within the first month.

key_levers.lever: Sleep consistency — fixed wake time seven days a week
key_levers.why_it_matters: Sleep is the single most controllable and consistently reported migraine trigger. The circadian rhythm drives hypothalamic regulation, which directly affects migraine threshold. A fixed wake time — even on weekends — anchors the circadian clock more reliably than a fixed bedtime.
key_levers.how_to_start: Choose a realistic consistent wake time. Set a single alarm and commit to it for 4 weeks. Track attack frequency before and after. Do not lie in even after a bad night or an attack.
key_levers.timeline: Improvements in attack frequency often visible within 4–6 weeks.

key_levers.why_it_matters: People who track their attacks identify patterns and triggers that are invisible to them in retrospect. The diary also provides the data needed for accurate diagnosis, medication decision-making, and assessing preventive treatment success.
key_levers.how_to_start: Download a dedicated app (Migraine Buddy is the most feature-complete; N1-Headache is more clinically validated) or start a simple paper log. Track: date, time of onset, duration, pain score, associated symptoms, potential triggers, and medications taken.
key_levers.timeline: Meaningful patterns typically emerge after 4–8 weeks of consistent tracking.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Medication overuse headache may be the primary driver of chronification in a significant proportion of people with frequent headache. Reversing MOH almost always results in substantial reduction in headache days — often more dramatic than any added medication.
key_levers.how_to_start: Count acute medication days per month using the headache diary. If triptans are used >10 days/month or analgesics >15 days/month, discuss MOH with a headache specialist. Supervised withdrawal is safer and more successful than attempting it alone.
key_levers.timeline: Withdrawal period typically 4–12 weeks. Improvement often begins within weeks of completing withdrawal.

key_levers.lever: Supplementation with riboflavin, magnesium, and CoQ10
key_levers.why_it_matters: These three supplements have the strongest evidence base for preventive effect and are recommended in multiple international headache society guidelines. They are safe, low-cost, and can be started independently. Their combined impact may be additive.
key_levers.how_to_start: Start riboflavin 400mg/day (any B2 supplement), magnesium glycinate or citrate 300–400mg elemental magnesium per day, and CoQ10 (ubiquinol form) 300mg/day. Track attacks for 3 months to assess response.
key_levers.timeline: 3 months minimum to assess preventive benefit for all three.

key_levers.lever: Stress regulation through a sustainable daily practice
key_levers.why_it_matters: Chronic stress sustains sympathetic nervous system activation and HPA axis overload, both of which lower the migraine threshold. The 'let-down' phenomenon (attacks on the first day of holiday, weekend, or after a deadline) is a distinct stress-related trigger requiring its own management strategy.
key_levers.how_to_start: Choose one practice sustainable daily: progressive muscle relaxation (15 minutes/day, available via free audio guides), diaphragmatic breathing practice, or a formal mindfulness programme. Consistency matters more than duration. A psychologist trained in pain management or headache CBT can provide structured guidance.
key_levers.timeline: Stress reduction effects on migraine typically emerge after 6–8 weeks of consistent practice.

common_mistakes: Trialling a preventive medication for only 4–6 weeks and concluding it 'doesn't work' — most preventives require 8–12 weeks at adequate dose to show full effect
Focusing exclusively on acute treatment (taking the right pill fast enough) while ignoring preventive options — this approach inevitably leads to more frequent attacks over time
Treating every prodrome with acute medication, accelerating the path to MOH and potentially aborting attacks that might not have fully developed
Eliminating too many dietary triggers at once and then being unable to identify which changes made a difference — systematic, one-at-a-time elimination is far more informative
Assuming that because one triptan or one preventive didn't work, the whole class is ineffective — significant individual variation exists within both triptan and preventive categories
Waiting for a 'perfect' stress management or sleep solution before starting — small consistent changes compound significantly; perfectionism is itself a migraine risk factor
Not monitoring and capping acute medication days — without a daily diary, medication overuse develops gradually and invisibly
Seeking only a neurologist or GP and not engaging a headache psychologist, which leaves the psychological, behavioural, and biofeedback components — all with strong evidence — unaddressed

specialists.role: Provides accurate diagnosis, manages preventive treatment escalation including CGRP antibodies, administers Botox for chronic migraine, and manages complex or refractory cases.
specialists.notes: Access to a dedicated headache neurologist (rather than a general neurologist) makes a significant difference. Headache medicine is a recognised subspecialty — look for a physician listed with the American Headache Society, British Association for the Study of Headache, or equivalent national body.

specialists.role: First point of contact for diagnosis and initial acute and preventive management. Can prescribe triptans and first-line preventives, refer to neurology, and monitor for MOH.
specialists.notes: GP knowledge of current migraine guidelines (including CGRP options and MOH) varies considerably. People who research their condition and arrive with a headache diary are better positioned to advocate for appropriate treatment.

specialists.role: Provides CBT, biofeedback, relaxation training, and psychological support for the disability, anticipatory anxiety, and behavioural components of chronic migraine.
specialists.notes: Strongly underutilised component of migraine care. CBT and biofeedback have evidence levels comparable to preventive medications. Most effective when combined with pharmacological management.

specialists.role: For women whose migraine is clearly hormonally driven — particularly menstrual migraine, perimenopausal migraine, or those needing guidance on contraception and HRT choices that minimise migraine impact.
specialists.notes: Combined oral contraceptives with oestrogen can worsen migraine with aura and carry cardiovascular risk. Progestogen-only contraception or non-hormonal methods are typically preferred. HRT decisions in perimenopause require careful discussion of oestrogen delivery method and stability of levels.

specialists.role: For suspected vestibular migraine or when the differential diagnosis includes Ménière's disease or other vestibular disorders.
specialists.notes: Vestibular migraine is frequently misdiagnosed for years. An otoneurologist or a neurologist with vestibular expertise is the appropriate specialist to navigate this diagnosis.

specialists.type: Functional medicine doctor or integrative neurologist
specialists.role: Investigates root causes and contributing factors not routinely explored in standard care — including nutrient deficiencies, mitochondrial function, hormonal balance, gut-brain axis, MTHFR variants, and inflammatory burden.
specialists.notes: Evidence quality varies for many integrative approaches. Most useful as a complement to, not replacement for, evidence-based headache care. Particularly valuable for people with multiple overlapping conditions or who have not responded to standard preventives.

patient_communities.name: r/migraine
patient_communities.notes: Large, active community of people living with migraine sharing treatment experiences, medication comparisons (especially for CGRP antibodies), and practical strategies. Useful for understanding real-world experiences with specific treatments.

patient_communities.name: The Migraine Trust Forum
patient_communities.notes: UK charity with a patient forum, detailed evidence-based information, and helpline. Particularly strong on UK treatment access and NICE guidance.

patient_communities.name: American Migraine Foundation Patient Community
patient_communities.notes: US-based foundation with extensive patient education resources, a 'Find a Doctor' tool for headache specialists, and community forums. Strong emphasis on evidence-based information.

patient_communities.name: Migraine Strong
patient_communities.notes: Community founded by people with chronic migraine, focused on practical management strategies, treatment updates, and peer support. Known for balanced, evidence-informed approach.

patient_communities.name: Miles for Migraine
patient_communities.notes: US-based patient advocacy organisation that runs community events connecting people with migraine to each other and to headache specialists. Valuable for finding specialist providers.

learning_resources.title: The Migraine Brain: Your Breakthrough Guide to Fewer, Less Painful Attacks
learning_resources.description: Written by a neurologist who has migraine herself. Accessible, comprehensive overview of the neuroscience, triggers, treatment options, and lifestyle strategies. Well suited to people who want to understand the 'why' behind their condition.

learning_resources.title: Heal Your Headache: The 1-2-3 Program for Taking Charge of Your Pain
learning_resources.description: Presents a systematic framework for managing migraine through trigger reduction and preventive medication. The dietary trigger section is detailed and practical. Some of the medication recommendations are dated but the trigger framework remains clinically useful.

learning_resources.title: American Migraine Foundation — Resource Library
learning_resources.description: Comprehensive, evidence-based patient education library covering all aspects of migraine — types, treatments (including CGRP therapies), lifestyle management, comorbidities, and how to work with headache specialists. Regularly updated.

learning_resources.title: Heads Up — Podcast by the American Headache Society
learning_resources.description: Interviews with headache specialists covering clinical topics including new therapies, comorbidities, special populations (pregnancy, menstrual migraine), and practical management. Pitched at an intermediate level accessible to engaged patients.

learning_resources.title: My Migraine Life — Blog and Podcast
learning_resources.description: Patient-led resource with interviews with headache specialists and other people with chronic migraine. Particularly useful for understanding the lived experience of navigating the healthcare system and accessing newer treatments.

learning_resources.title: ICHD-3 Diagnostic Criteria for Migraine (full text)
learning_resources.description: The official diagnostic criteria for all migraine subtypes. Reading these criteria helps individuals understand exactly what clinicians are assessing for and how to communicate their symptoms accurately during consultations.

learning_resources.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide and Migraine — Review of the Science
learning_resources.author_or_source: Edvinsson et al., Nature Reviews Neurology
learning_resources.description: Detailed scientific review of the CGRP pathway in migraine pathophysiology and the development of CGRP-targeted therapies. Appropriate for those wanting to understand the mechanism behind the newest generation of migraine treatments.

learning_resources.title: Migraine Buddy App
learning_resources.description: The most widely used migraine tracking app. Captures detailed attack data including triggers, symptoms, medications, and pain levels. Generates reports useful for clinical appointments. Free core version is sufficient for most purposes.
